name: master italian tomato sauce and pesto
instructions:
A progression of italian pasta sauces built around tomato, from the simplest cooked sauce to the full pesto. Read the variants in order — each is a stepping stone toward the next. For green-sauce/pesto equivalents (basil pesto and its blender-vs-pestle variants), see master-italian-green-sauce-and-pesto.

=== VARIANT A — MARCELLA HAZAN'S 3-INGREDIENT TOMATO SAUCE (THE STARTER) ===

Marcella Hazan's famous three-ingredient sauce. The simplest possible italian tomato sauce; canonical starting point.

INGREDIENTS:
- 28-oz can whole peeled tomatoes (San Marzano ideal — canned whole, not diced, not paste)
- 1 medium yellow onion, peeled and halved (NOT chopped — kept in halves)
- 5 tablespoons unsalted butter
- salt to taste

PROCEDURE:
1. Combine tomatoes, butter, onion halves, and a pinch of salt in a saucepan.
2. Simmer uncovered on low for ~45 minutes, stirring occasionally and crushing the tomatoes with the back of a spoon as they soften.
3. Fish out and discard the onion halves before serving (they've done their aromatic job).
4. Toss with cooked pasta.

=== VARIANT B — HALFWAY-PESTO (BLENDED PASTA SAUCE) ===

Halfway between Hazan's sauce and the full sicilian pesto. Adds pesto-shaped ingredients on top of the Variant A base, then blends smooth with an immersion blender. Still intended for pasta but reads closer to a spread — think romesco without bell peppers.

INGREDIENTS:
- 28-oz can whole peeled tomatoes
- 1 medium yellow onion (halved OR chopped — see notes)
- 2 tablespoons butter (for flavor) + 2-3 tablespoons olive oil (for bulk) — see notes
- 3-4 cloves garlic
- dried oregano
- black pepper
- red wine vinegar (a splash to finish)
- grated parmesan cheese
- 1/3 to 1/2 cup nuts/seeds: almonds, sunflower seeds, walnuts (one or a mix)
- red chili flakes (pinch — see notes)
- salt to taste
- pasta (short shapes work best)

PROCEDURE:
1. Combine tomatoes, onion, butter/oil, garlic, oregano, black pepper, red chili flakes, and nuts/seeds in a saucepan.
2. Simmer ~30-40 minutes to cook down and soften everything.
3. Take off heat. Immersion-blend directly in the pot until smooth (or transferred to a countertop blender).
4. Stir in a splash of red wine vinegar, the grated parmesan, and salt to taste.
5. Toss with cooked pasta. Loosen with pasta water if too thick.

=== VARIANT C — TRADITIONAL SICILIAN TOMATO PESTO (PESTO ROSSO ALLA TRAPANESE) ===

The full canonical form. No cooking — a raw blended pesto.

INGREDIENTS:
- sun-dried tomatoes (rehydrated in warm water if dry-packed; drain and pat dry)
- almonds (blanched, unsalted)
- fresh basil leaves
- garlic (raw, 1-2 cloves)
- olive oil (good quality)
- pecorino cheese (traditional) or parmesan cheese
- salt
- red chili flakes (optional, traditional in some sicilian versions)
- pasta (short shapes like busiate traditionally; penne, fusilli, or trofie all work)

PROCEDURE:
1. Rehydrate the sun-dried tomatoes if dry-packed. Skip if oil-packed.
2. In a food processor or blender, combine sun-dried tomatoes, almonds, basil, garlic, and a pinch of red chili flakes. Pulse to a rough paste.
3. With the motor running, drizzle in olive oil until the sauce reaches a thick, spreadable consistency — a loose paste, not a smooth sauce.
4. Fold in grated pecorino (or parmesan) and salt to taste. Fold in by hand off the blades — don't blend the cheese.
5. Toss with hot cooked pasta. Loosen with a splash of pasta water if too thick.

(LATER: variant D — arrabbiata — to be added. Not written yet.)
notes:
THE PROGRESSION — SIMPLE COOKED SAUCE → HALFWAY-BLENDED → RAW PESTO: the three variants form a ladder of complexity and processing. A is the simplest cooked sauce (three ingredients, chunky, no blending). B builds on A's base but adds the pesto-shaped ingredients and blends smooth (still cooked, but reads much closer to a spread than a sauce). C drops cooking entirely and uses sun-dried tomatoes for the tomato-forward register (raw blended pesto proper). Reading them in order helps you understand what each one is doing and when to reach for which.

=== VARIANT A NOTES ===

WHY IT WORKS WITH ONLY THREE INGREDIENTS: Hazan's insight is that a whole peeled canned tomato + butter + onion, given enough time (~45 min uncovered), builds a sauce that reads more complex than the ingredient list suggests. Butter adds richness and rounds acidity in a way olive oil can't at this simplicity. The onion halves perfume the sauce without becoming a chunky component — that's why they go in whole and come out before serving. No garlic, no basil, no oregano, no chili — the restraint is the recipe.

DON'T CHOP THE ONION IN VARIANT A: the onion halves are meant to be discarded. Chopping the onion turns it into part of the sauce texture and changes the dish. If you want chopped onion in the sauce, that's Variant B territory.

UNSALTED BUTTER, GENEROUS: Hazan calls for 5 tablespoons per 28-oz can. Don't cut it — the butter is doing structural work, not just flavor. Unsalted so you control the seasoning.

SAN MARZANO IF POSSIBLE: whole peeled San Marzano tomatoes are what the recipe was tuned to. Other whole peeled canned tomatoes work; diced canned tomatoes read watery here.

=== VARIANT B NOTES ===

WHAT B IS FOR — SIMPLE + FLAVOR-STACKED, BUT NOT PESTO YET: variant B is what you make when Hazan's sauce feels too plain but you don't have sun-dried tomatoes for the full pesto. It borrows the pesto-shaped ingredients (garlic, oregano, parmesan, nuts, vinegar) but keeps the canned-tomato base and the cooked simmer. The immersion-blend at the end smooths it into a spreadable pasta sauce that reads richer and more layered than A.

BUTTER + OLIVE OIL — BOTH, NOT ONE OR THE OTHER: use butter for flavor and olive oil for bulk. A couple tablespoons of butter carries the rounded rich character that hazan's variant A leans on; the additional olive oil supplies the volume of fat the blended sauce actually needs without over-buttering the dish. Using butter alone at the required volume reads heavy; using olive oil alone reads flat and misses the flavor the butter brings. The split is the answer.

ROMESCO WITHOUT BELL PEPPERS COMPARISON: variant B ends up structurally similar to a romesco (blended sauce of tomato + nuts + garlic + vinegar + oil) minus the roasted bell peppers. If you like romesco and want a pasta version, this is that dish. The nut/seed choice is where the biggest character shifts live — almonds are romesco-forward, walnuts are earthier and more autumnal, sunflower seeds are the cheapest and most neutral.

OREGANO IS THE HERB, NOT BASIL: basil belongs in variant C (raw, fresh, off-heat). Variant B is cooked, and dried oregano's earthy warmth stands up to the simmer where basil would just die. Save basil for the raw pesto.

SPLASH OF RED WINE VINEGAR TO FINISH: the vinegar goes in AFTER blending, off heat. Adds the acid brightness that pushes the sauce toward pesto/romesco territory rather than plain tomato sauce. Red wine vinegar specifically — white wine vinegar reads too sharp here.

NUT/SEED FLEXIBILITY — ALMONDS, WALNUTS, SUNFLOWER SEEDS: any one or a mix. Almonds are the most traditional-pesto-leaning; walnuts push the sauce toward an autumnal/rustic register; sunflower seeds are the neutral budget option. Skip pine nuts — those belong in green pesto.

RED CHILI FLAKES — A REAL PART OF THE FLAVOR STACK: a pinch of red chili flakes goes in with the simmering base, not sprinkled on top at the end. Adds the vegetal heat that pushes B further toward romesco territory (romesco carries that same background pepper warmth). Keep it a pinch — this isn't arrabbiata, and the heat should land as depth, not as a heat headline.

=== VARIANT C NOTES ===

SUN-DRIED TOMATOES ARE THE STRUCTURAL INGREDIENT — NOT TOMATO PASTE, NOT CANNED: the tomato base for variant C is specifically sun-dried tomatoes. Their chewy, umami-forward, slightly sweet dried-fruit register is what makes this read as pesto rather than sauce. Tomato paste is too cooked/concentrated; canned tomatoes carry too much water for a raw blended paste. Both belong in the earlier variants, not here.

ALMONDS ARE THE NUT — BLANCHED, UNSALTED: pine nuts are for green pesto; almonds anchor red. Blanched (skins off) gives the cleanest color. Unsalted so you control the seasoning.

PINE NUTS ARE CLASHING — DIFFERENT PESTO ENTIRELY: pine nuts belong in green pesto (see master-italian-green-sauce-and-pesto) where their delicate resiny sweetness bridges basil and parmesan. In a tomato base they get lost.

BASIL IS NON-OPTIONAL IN VARIANT C: without fresh basil the dish reads underwhelming — just ground sun-dried tomatoes and almonds with cheese. Basil is what makes it a pesto rather than an italian-tomato-and-almond spread.

PECORINO IS TRADITIONAL; PARMESAN IS FINE: the classic sicilian version uses pecorino — sharper, saltier, sheep's-milk depth against the sweet sun-dried tomatoes. Parmesan works too and is more likely to be on hand; pecorino is the upgrade when you have it.

FOOD PROCESSOR OR BLENDER, NOT MORTAR: red pesto wants a blender or food processor. Almonds are too hard to break down by hand with a pestle in reasonable time. Green pesto is a mortar dish; red pesto is a blender dish.

SHORT PASTA SHAPES: sicilian tradition pairs pesto trapanese with busiate (a twisted pasta). Penne, fusilli, trofie, cavatelli, or orecchiette all work. Long strands (spaghetti, linguine) don't grip red pesto well.

SUNFLOWER SEEDS AS OPTIONAL ADDITION: sunflower seeds work alongside almonds if you want more nut/seed volume — see the nuts-and-seeds article on red-pesto structural pairings. Not traditional in variant C, but a real move.

=== SHARED / CROSS-VARIANT NOTES ===

TOMATO PASTE IS CLASHING ACROSS ALL VARIANTS: none of the three variants want tomato paste. A wants canned whole tomatoes; B wants canned whole tomatoes; C wants sun-dried. Paste's concentrated cooked-canned character doesn't fit anywhere in this family.

SHORT PASTA SHAPES ACROSS THE BOARD: all three variants prefer short pasta shapes (penne, fusilli, rigatoni) over long strands. The sauces are all thick or chunky enough that spaghetti/linguine slide out from under them.

GREAT FOR AN OVEN SKILLET PASTA BAKE (VARIANTS B AND C ESPECIALLY): if the sauce carries some kind of fat — nuts or seeds — it makes a great base for an oven skillet pasta bake. Variants B and C both bring almonds/walnuts/sunflower seeds and cheese, which give the sauce the fat and body it needs to stand up to a hot oven bake without drying out. Variant A (Hazan) is too lean — the butter cooks off in the oven and the sauce reads dry. Two things to watch when using these in a bake:
- FRESH BASIL GARNISH GOES ON AFTER THE BAKE, NOT BEFORE: any fresh basil should be scattered on top after the pasta comes out of the oven. Basil baked into the pasta oxidizes to a stale-brown color and loses its flavor — same rule as basil in a sauté (see italian stuffed peppers). Reserve some fresh basil separately from the sauce and add off-heat.
- BREADCRUMB OR CHEESE TOP FOR BROWNING: the bake benefits from a top layer that browns — grated parmesan, pecorino, or breadcrumbs (or both) scattered over the pasta before the oven finish. Gives you the crispy top that separates a pasta bake from just baked pasta.
